content.text: Artifacts are one of the most useful features in Claude.ai, turning Claude into not just a conversational assistant but a collaborative creative and technical tool. When you ask Claude to produce substantial content — a web page, a script, a document, a visualization — an artifact is a dedicated display area where that content lives, separate from the conversation thread. Think of it as Claude opening a working document alongside your chat where it can build, show, and update real deliverables.
What Exactly Is an Artifact

An artifact is a self-contained piece of content generated by Claude that appears in a separate panel in the Claude.ai interface, distinct from the normal chat messages. While a standard chat message is text that flows in the conversation, an artifact is a structured, often rendered piece of work — a website, a script, a document — that has its own display space on the right side of the screen.

The artifact panel serves as a live preview and editing canvas. For HTML and React artifacts, the panel renders the content as an actual interactive webpage. For code, it shows syntax-highlighted source. For markdown, it shows the formatted document. This visual distinction between 'talking about' something and 'actually building' something is what makes artifacts so valuable — you are not just discussing a concept, you are producing a tangible deliverable.

Artifacts are persistent within a conversation. If you ask Claude to update or revise an artifact, it modifies the artifact in place rather than creating a new one in the chat. This means you always see the current, latest version of what you are working on without having to scroll through old messages. You can ask Claude to iterate on an artifact dozens of times, and the panel always shows the most up-to-date version.
Why Artifacts Were Created

Before the artifacts feature existed, working with Claude on code or documents meant receiving outputs as blocks of text inside chat messages. You would copy the code, paste it into your editor, ask Claude for a revision, and repeat the copy-paste cycle over and over. This workflow was workable but tedious, and it obscured the fact that you were iterating on a single piece of work — each new version appeared as a separate message, making it hard to see the evolution of the document.
Focused workspace: Artifacts give generated content its own space, keeping the conversation thread clean and readable.
Live rendering: HTML and React artifacts render as real interactive pages in the panel, giving immediate visual feedback.
In-place updates: Claude modifies the same artifact across revisions rather than generating a new output each time.
Quick copy and export: Dedicated copy and download controls in the panel make it easy to export the finished content.
Professional presentation: An artifact looks like a finished deliverable, not a raw block of code pasted into a chat window.
Iterative refinement: The artifact model naturally encourages an iterative workflow — draft, review, revise, repeat.
Types of Artifacts
HTML artifacts: Complete HTML documents rendered as live web pages in the panel. You can click buttons, fill forms, and interact with the content.
React artifacts: React component code rendered live in the panel using a built-in runtime. Ideal for UI prototypes and interactive data displays.
Code artifacts: Source code in any programming language, displayed with syntax highlighting. Supports Python, JavaScript, SQL, Bash, and many more.
Markdown artifacts: Documents formatted with markdown — headings, bullet lists, tables, and bold/italic text rendered as a polished document.
SVG artifacts: Scalable vector graphics rendered as actual images in the panel. Useful for diagrams, logos, charts, and illustrations.
Plain text artifacts: Unformatted text content such as configuration files, data files, or prose documents.
Mermaid diagram artifacts: Flowcharts, sequence diagrams, and entity relationship diagrams rendered from Mermaid syntax.
How Artifacts Work

When you ask Claude for something substantial — a web page, a script, a detailed document — Claude evaluates whether the output warrants an artifact. Artifacts are triggered by content that is meaningful as a standalone piece of work rather than just an explanation or a short snippet. A 10-line code example in an explanation stays in the chat; a complete 200-line script becomes an artifact.

Claude determines the artifact type based on the content. A response containing valid HTML gets an HTML artifact. Python code gets a code artifact. A structured document gets a markdown artifact. Claude can also be explicitly asked to create an artifact of a specific type, and it will format the content appropriately.

Once created, the artifact is updated through subsequent requests in the conversation. You can say 'add a dark mode toggle to the page' or 'add error handling to the function' and Claude will modify the artifact in place. The conversation thread records your requests and Claude's explanations of changes made, while the artifact panel shows the actual current state of the work.
Step-by-Step: Getting Started with Artifacts
Sign in to Claude.ai: Go to claude.ai and sign in. Artifacts work on both free and paid plans, with some feature differences between tiers.
Start a new conversation: Click 'New Chat' or start typing in the input area. Artifacts can be created in any conversation.

Ask Claude to build something: Use prompts that request a complete, standalone piece of content. Examples: 'Build a complete HTML page for a portfolio website', 'Write a Python script that scrapes weather data', or 'Create a markdown report on renewable energy trends'.

Watch the artifact panel appear: Claude will generate the content and display it in the artifact panel on the right side of the screen. The panel may show a loading state while Claude generates the output.

Interact with the artifact: For HTML, click through the page. For code, read the source. For markdown, read the formatted document. Use the toggle to switch between source and rendered views if available.
Request revisions: Type in the chat to ask Claude to modify the artifact. Be specific about what you want changed. Claude will update the artifact and explain what it changed in the conversation.
Copy or download when done: Use the copy button in the artifact panel to copy the content to your clipboard, or use the download button if available for your artifact type.
Practical Examples of Artifacts in Action

A freelance web developer uses artifacts to prototype landing pages for clients. They describe the client's business and brand, ask Claude to create a full HTML landing page, and get a rendered preview in seconds. They share the preview with the client using the share feature, collect feedback, and ask Claude to make revisions — all without touching a code editor. For simple client sites, the entire design iteration happens in Claude.ai.

A data scientist uses code artifacts to quickly build data processing scripts. They describe the dataset format and the analysis they need, ask Claude to write the Python script as an artifact, review the code in the panel, and then copy it to their local environment. When they find an edge case that the script does not handle, they paste the error back into the chat and Claude updates the artifact with a fix.

A content marketer uses markdown artifacts to draft long-form blog posts. They give Claude a topic, target audience, and key points to cover, and Claude produces a structured article as a markdown artifact. The marketer reviews it in the rendered panel, asks for tonal adjustments and specific additions, and downloads the final version as a file when ready.

A teacher uses React artifacts to build interactive quizzes for students. They describe the quiz structure and questions, ask Claude to create a React component, and see a live, functioning quiz in the preview panel. They test it themselves, ask for additions like a timer or scoring, and when satisfied, share the artifact link directly with students.
When to Request an Artifact
When you need a complete, standalone file — not an explanation or snippet, but an actual deliverable you will use or share.
When you will iterate on the content across multiple rounds of feedback and revision.
When the content benefits from visual rendering, such as HTML pages, React components, or diagrams.
When you want to quickly share the result with someone else without exporting or hosting anything.
When building something through conversation — a prototype, a document, a tool — where maintaining the latest state in a dedicated panel is helpful.
Limitations and Trade-offs
Artifacts only exist within their conversation and are not independently stored in a library you can browse.
HTML artifacts run in a browser sandbox and cannot access external URLs, your file system, or browser extensions.
Complex multi-file projects cannot be fully represented as a single artifact — you may need multiple artifacts or a code editor for large projects.
Artifacts are a Claude.ai web feature and are not available through the API in the same interactive form.
Rendering fidelity for complex HTML may differ slightly from a production browser environment due to the sandboxed preview.
Tips and Best Practices
Ask for 'a complete [type of artifact]' to reliably trigger artifact creation rather than an inline response.
Review the artifact before copying — Claude sometimes makes minor formatting choices that may not match your style; it is easy to spot these in the rendered view.
Use the conversation thread to discuss what you want and the artifact panel to track what you have — keep them in their intended roles.
For large projects, break them into sections and build artifacts for each, then combine them at the end.
Test interactive artifacts thoroughly in the panel before sharing — verify all buttons, forms, and interactions work as expected.
For code artifacts that will run in your environment, check imports and dependencies before executing — Claude generates correct code for the request but you need the relevant libraries installed.
Use artifacts to create templates you reuse — ask Claude to create a general-purpose HTML template or a report structure that you can adapt for different content.
Combine the 'share' feature with artifacts to get rapid feedback from stakeholders without any file transfer or hosting overhead.
Common Questions
Is an artifact the same as an attachment?

No. Attachments are files you upload to Claude — documents, images, PDFs — that you provide as input for Claude to read and analyze. Artifacts are outputs that Claude creates for you. You upload attachments to give Claude context; Claude creates artifacts to give you deliverables. The two features serve opposite directions of information flow — attachments go in, artifacts come out.
Can I edit an artifact directly without asking Claude?

In the current Claude.ai interface, the artifact panel is primarily a display and preview area rather than a direct editing environment. You interact with the artifact indirectly by asking Claude to make changes in the conversation. Some future interface updates or specific artifact types may allow more direct editing, but the primary workflow is to request changes through conversation and have Claude update the artifact.
How many artifacts can exist in one conversation?

A single conversation can contain multiple artifacts — one for each distinct deliverable you ask Claude to create. For example, you might create an HTML artifact for a webpage, a CSS artifact for a stylesheet, and a Python artifact for a backend script, all within the same conversation. You can navigate between artifacts in the panel. Each artifact is tracked and updated independently, so you can ask Claude to modify any of them at any point.

Overview

What Are Artifacts is a fundamental concept for anyone working with Claude and the Anthropic ecosystem. Understanding it deeply allows you to build more effective applications, write better prompts, and get more value from Claude's capabilities. This section provides a thorough exploration of the topic, covering both the theory and practical application aspects that you'll encounter in real-world usage.

Whether you're a beginner just starting out or an experienced developer looking to deepen your knowledge, this guide walks through everything you need to know about What Are Artifacts. We'll cover the core concepts, walk through concrete examples, address common questions, and show you how to apply what you've learned in practical scenarios.

Practical Examples
The best way to understand What Are Artifacts is through concrete examples. These examples range from simple to complex, illustrating different aspects of the topic.
Basic Example
The following example demonstrates the most fundamental use case for What Are Artifacts. This is a great starting point for understanding the core behavior.

# Example: Getting started with What Are Artifacts
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Basic usage example
message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Explain What Are Artifacts in simple terms with a practical example."
        }
    ]
)

print(message.content[0].text)
Advanced Example
This more advanced example shows how to use What Are Artifacts in a real-world scenario with proper error handling, configuration, and best practices applied.

# Advanced example: Production-ready What Are Artifacts implementation
import anthropic
from typing import Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ClaudeWhatareartifactsHandler:
    """Handles What Are Artifacts with production best practices."""

    def __init__(self, api_key: Optional[str] = None):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)
        self.model = "claude-opus-4-5"

    def process(self, input_text: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str:
        """Process input with error handling and logging."""
        try:
            logger.info(f"Processing request of length {len(input_text)}")
            response = self.client.messages.create(
                model=self.model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]
            )
            result = response.content[0].text
            logger.info(f"Successfully generated response of length {len(result)}")
            return result
        except anthropic.RateLimitError:
            logger.warning("Rate limit reached, consider reducing request frequency")
            raise
        except anthropic.APIError as e:
            logger.error(f"API error: {e}")
            raise

# Usage
handler = ClaudeWhatareartifactsHandler()
result = handler.process("Your query here")
print(result)

Frequently Asked Questions
These are the most common questions about What Are Artifacts, based on developer feedback and support requests.
Q: What is the most important thing to understand about What Are Artifacts?

A: The most important thing to understand is that What Are Artifacts is a system that rewards careful, intentional usage. The more precisely you communicate your intent, the better results you'll get. Start with clear, specific inputs and refine based on the outputs you receive.
Q: How does this interact with other Claude features?

A: What Are Artifacts works together with most other Claude features. It's particularly important to understand how it interacts with system prompts, conversation history, and any tools or integrations you're using. In most cases, these interactions are synergistic rather than conflicting.
Q: What are the performance implications?

A: Performance depends on several factors: the complexity of your inputs, the length of your context window, and the Claude model you're using. Generally, simpler and more focused inputs produce faster and more accurate results. For high-throughput use cases, consider batching and caching strategies.
Q: How do I debug issues?

A: When debugging What Are Artifacts issues, start by isolating the problem. Simplify your input to the minimum that reproduces the issue, then gradually add complexity back. Check the API documentation for current behavior specifications and use logging to capture the full request-response cycle.
